items.title: Is Angi Worth It for Contractors? The Real Cost Per Booked Job
items.blurb: Is Angi worth it? The honest math: leads get shared with up to 12 pros, so your real cost per booked job runs ~$1,430–2,500 — vs ~$290 organic. Here's the full breakdown, plus a free calculator that shows YOUR real cost per Angi lead. 👇
items.content_html:
Every contractor asks the same question eventually: is Angi actually worth it? The honest answer, once you run the math, is that for most established shops it isn't — and the reason is the shared-lead model.

Angi (and HomeAdvisor, and Thumbtack) sells the same lead to as many as 5–12 contractors. So while an individual lead looks cheap at $15–$100, your real cost per booked job climbs to roughly $1,430–$2,500 once you account for the low close rates on leads you're racing other trucks to win. Compare that to about $290 for a job won through your own SEO or referrals.

The deeper problem isn't that Angi never produces work — it's that the day you stop paying, the leads stop. You rented an audience instead of building one.

We broke the whole thing down — including a free calculator that shows your real cost per Angi lead based on your own close rate and ticket size — over on Booked Job:

→ Read the full breakdown: Is Angi Worth It for Contractors?

Booked Job is an independent resource for home-service business owners — plumbers, roofers, HVAC techs and electricians — on getting more booked jobs without getting ripped off.

items.title: How to Price a Home-Service Job (Without Leaving Money on the Table)
items.blurb: Most contractors don't lose money on the work — they lose it on the estimate. The fix: price off margin, not markup (a 50% markup is only a 33% margin). Here's the money math nobody teaches you in the apprenticeship, plus a free calculator for what to actually charge.
items.content_html:
Most contractors don't lose money because they're bad at the work. They lose it on the estimate — pricing off gut feel and doing markup math that quietly leaves profit on the table every job.

The big one: markup and margin are not the same number. A 50% markup on a $100 cost gets you a $150 price — but that's only a 33% margin. To actually keep 50%, you'd charge $200. Across a year of estimates, that gap is real money.

The fix is one formula: take your direct cost (materials + labor + subs) and divide by (1 − your target margin). A $400 job at a 55% target margin should be priced around $889.

We broke down the three numbers every price is built on — plus a free margin calculator that tells you what to charge — on Booked Job:

→ Read the full breakdown: How to Price a Job

Booked Job is an independent resource for home-service business owners on running a more profitable shop.

items.title: How to Get More Google Reviews for Your Service Business
items.blurb: Reviews decide who shows up in the map pack and who gets the call. The system: ask in person the second the job's done, then text the direct review link within the hour — one tap. Ask every customer (gating is against Google's rules), and aim for a steady trickle, not a burst.
items.content_html:
Reviews are the cheapest marketing you'll ever do — they decide who shows up in the map pack and who gets the call. Yet most shops “mean to ask” and never do.

The fix is a system, not motivation: ask in person the second the job's done and the customer's happy, then text them the direct review link within the hour, while the goodwill is hot. Make it one tap — don't make them search for your business.

Two rules keep you out of trouble: ask every customer (only asking the happy ones is “review gating,” against Google's policy), and never offer a discount or gift for a review (incentivized reviews can get your profile suspended).

We laid out the full timing-and-tools system on Booked Job:

→ Read it: How to Get More Google Reviews

Booked Job is an independent resource for the trades on getting found and chosen online.

items.title: Surviving the Slow Season: A Cash-Flow Playbook for the Trades
items.blurb: The slow season is won in the busy season. Bank a 3–6 month cushion while work flows, sell maintenance plans and weather-independent jobs, re-engage past customers, and INCREASE marketing while competitors go quiet. The shops that struggle assume every good month lasts forever.
items.content_html:
Every trade has a slow stretch — the dead of winter, the post-holiday lull, the rainy run. It's not an emergency; it's a season you can see coming. The owners who sail through plan in July for what January will feel like.

The playbook: bank a 3–6 month cushion during peak months (sweep a fixed percent of every job into a separate account before it feels spendable); sell the work that ignores weather — maintenance plans, opposite-season installs, tune-ups; and mine your past customers, your cheapest pipeline by far.

And don't go dark on marketing. The slow season is exactly when to keep or increase it — competitors pull back, attention gets cheaper, and you head into the next busy stretch with a full pipeline while everyone else starts from zero.

→ Read the full playbook: Surviving the Slow Season

Booked Job is an independent resource for home-service business owners on building a business that doesn't panic when the phone slows down.

items.title: What Marketing Actually Is for Contractors (No-BS Guide)
items.blurb: Marketing isn't ads or a logo. It's a machine that takes a stranger with a problem and turns them into a paid job: get found, get picked, get booked. Everything else is decoration.

items.title: CPL vs CPBJ: The 2 Numbers That Run Your Marketing
items.blurb: CPL (cost per lead) is the bait; CPBJ (cost per booked job) is the meal. A cheap lead that never books is the most expensive lead there is. Always judge a source by CPBJ.

items.title: Where Customers Actually Find Contractors (The 5 Doors)
items.blurb: Customers come through five doors: your Google profile, your website, paid ads, lead apps, and reviews. Two are free and come first. Living behind one door is a time bomb.

items.title: Google Business Profile for Contractors: Win the Map Pack
items.blurb: The map pack is free real estate at the top of local search with only 3 spots. Your Google Business Profile is what gets you in. Claim it, pick the right category, add real photos.

items.title: Why 98% of People Leave Your Contractor Website (and the Fix)
items.blurb: A contractor website has one job: turn a visitor into a call. Most fail — the average converts 2–3%. Make it fast, mobile, with a giant tap-to-call number, proof, and one clear action.

items.title: Contractor Reviews: Why They Decide Who Gets the Call
items.blurb: Before a customer hears your voice, they've judged you by your reviews. Count, recency, and your responses all matter — and a shop with 140 recent reviews beats one with 12, even at a higher price.

items.title: Should Contractors Pay for Leads? The Honest Math (LSA, Angi, Thumbtack)
items.blurb: Sometimes paid leads are worth it, sometimes a trap. LSA pays per real lead at high intent; shared apps put you in a footrace. Judge every source by cost per booked job, never the sticker price.

items.title: Google Ads for Contractors: Spend Your First Dollar Without Getting Burned
items.blurb: Search ads put you in front of someone searching for your trade right now. The fix to not getting burned: answer every call, start tiny with tight keywords, track by cost per booked job, kill the losers.

items.title: Get Found by Google AND AI: SEO + AEO for Contractors
items.blurb: Getting found used to mean Google. Now a third of people ask an AI tool who to hire. The same foundation — a strong profile, real reviews, a clear website that answers questions — wins both.

items.title: The Contractor Marketing Scorecard: 5 Numbers to Track Monthly
items.blurb: Track five numbers monthly: cost per lead, cost per booked job, close rate, review count, response time. Color each green/yellow/red. Knowing your numbers is what makes you impossible to fleece.

items.title: Speed to Lead for Contractors: Why You're Losing Jobs You Already Paid For
items.blurb: Customers hire the first contractor who responds, not the best one. If you don't answer or text back within about five minutes, you've likely lost the job — and if you paid for that lead, you just paid your competitor to win it.

items.title: Contractor Referrals & Repeat Customers: Your Cheapest Marketing
items.blurb: Your past customers are the cheapest, highest-closing leads you have — they already trust you. Most contractors never ask for the referral or follow up, so the work quietly goes to whoever does.

items.title: Google Local Services Ads: The Better Lead Math
items.blurb: Google Local Services Ads cost about $168 per booked job — far below Angi/HomeAdvisor's ~$542 (2026 lead-network comparisons) — because the leads are exclusive to you, not sold to 2-5 pros. By trade, SearchLight 2026 shows HVAC returns 9.55x, electrical 8.52x, and plumbing 6.85x on spend.

items.title: The "I Audited Your Site" Cold Pitch: A Contractor Scam Tell
items.blurb: If a stranger emails or cold-calls saying they "audited your site," that unsolicited contact is the #1 red flag. Reputable agencies grow on referrals and inbound, not by dialing contractors three states away. The "audit" is a mass form letter built to scare you onto a call. Ask for references, or just delete it.

items.title: \"Guaranteed #1 on Google\" Is Always a Lie — Here's Why
items.blurb: Google publicly says no one can guarantee a #1 ranking, because Google controls the results and changes them constantly. So any \"guaranteed #1 in 30 days\" pitch is a lie. Real local SEO is a slow monthly build with no promised date — anyone handing you one is making it up.

items.title: The \"Limited-Time SEO Offer\" Trick, Decoded
items.blurb: A countdown clock on an SEO deal is a sales trick, not a real deadline. Google's rankings take months to move, so signing today versus next week changes nothing about your results. The timer exists to stop you from asking the real question: what does this cost per booked job?

items.title: The "47 Errors" SEO Audit Scam Contractors Fall For
items.blurb: A canned scare-tactic audit is an auto-generated list of small site "errors" — missing meta descriptions, a few 404s, alt tags — dressed up to look critical so you sign a monthly contract. Most don't move rankings. The real tell: it never names the competitors actually beating you.

items.title: White-Label Markup: Is Your Agency Reselling Offshore Work?
items.blurb: White-label markup is when a local-looking agency sends your marketing work overseas, then resells it to you as senior in-house talent at a steep markup. Outsourcing isn't the scam — hiding it and overcharging is. Ask who does the work, where they sit, and who owns your accounts.

items.title: Fake Google Maps Listings: The Phantom Competitor Scam
items.blurb: Some agencies create fake Google Maps listings for contractors that don't exist, then catch the calls and resell them as leads. You end up paying for work that was already yours. Spot phantom pins by checking Street View and the phone number, then report them from your own verified profile.

items.title: The Free Marketing Demo That Turns Into a Surprise Bill
items.blurb: A free marketing demo is bait. The demo costs nothing, but the contract you sign at the end does — auto-renewal, a long lock-in term, an early-termination fee, and a website you may not even own. Read the fine print and ask: if I cancel next month, what do I keep?

items.title: The True Cost of Hiring a Marketing Agency (Contractors)
items.blurb: A marketing agency costs more than the retainer they quote. You also pay a setup fee, ad spend on top (that money goes to Google, not the agency), and sign a contract lock-in. Judge any agency by one number: cost per booked job versus your profit per job.

items.title: How to Spend Your First $10K in Marketing as a Contractor
items.blurb: Spend in priority order: first fix your free Google profile and pile up reviews, then build a website that actually books jobs, then turn on Google Local Services Ads — the cheapest booked job at about $168 (SearchLight Digital 2026). Skip shared-lead networks like Angi and Thumbtack. And answer your phone fast.

items.title: Reels for Contractors: Where to Spend Your Time in 2026
items.blurb: Short video (Reels, Shorts, TikTok) is the one place strangers who don't follow you still see your work. It reaches new homeowners in your town for free and pre-builds trust before they call — unlike paid leads that cost up to $542 a booked job (2026 data) and get shared with competitors.

items.title: Cost Per Booked Job: The One Marketing Formula
items.blurb: Judge every marketing offer by one number: cost per booked job. Take dollars spent and divide by jobs actually booked. Ignore "cost per lead" — a lead is just a name, not money. This single formula lets you compare any channel honestly and makes you impossible to fleece.

items.title: How Long SEO Really Takes for Contractors (Months)
items.blurb: SEO usually takes months to start booking jobs, not days, because Google ranks you on trust that builds slowly through reviews, local pages, and time. It's the cheapest job source long-term, but you need faster leads — Local Services Ads, paid search, and answering every call fast — running while it builds.

items.title: Yelp for Contractors: Yes or No? The Honest Cost Take
items.blurb: Claim your free Yelp listing — reviews matter. But think hard before buying Yelp ads. The cheapest booked job comes from Google LSA at ~$168 and it's exclusive (SearchLight 2026). Yelp ads usually sell shared leads, which convert far worse. Make Yelp beat your best option or skip it.

items.title: Real HVAC Marketing Numbers: Cost Per Booked Job
items.blurb: Per 2026 lead-network comparisons, a booked HVAC job costs about $168 on Google LSA, $250 on Thumbtack, and $542 on Angi/HomeAdvisor. LSA wins because the lead is exclusive — SearchLight 2026 shows $51 per lead, 44% book, a $2,110 ticket, and 9.55x ROAS for HVAC.

items.title: Thumbtack for Contractors: Is It Worth ~$250 a Job?
items.blurb: Thumbtack sells each lead to 4-5 pros and bills you per contact. Because shared leads close at just 6-10%, your real cost lands around $250 per booked job (2026 lead-network comparisons). It's worth it on high-ticket trades if you answer in under 5 minutes — and a money pit if you can't.

items.title: Angi & HomeAdvisor: The Real $542-Per-Job Cost
items.blurb: Angi and HomeAdvisor are the same company selling the same shared lead. A booked job costs about $542 (2026 lead-network comparisons) versus $168 on Google LSA. Fees run $300-500 a year, leads are shared 2-4 ways, and refunds come as credit, not cash. It works as a starter, not forever.

items.title: Marketing Agency Won't Share Access? Here's Why
items.blurb: If a marketing company won't give you admin access to your own Google Search Console, Analytics, ads, and website — or can't show what they did this month — you don't have a marketing partner. You have a hostage situation. You must own every account. They get invited in.

items.title: Why WordPress Is Wrong for Most Contractor Sites
items.blurb: WordPress was built to publish words, not turn a panicked homeowner into a phone call. It's slow with plugins, costs money to maintain and secure, and does nothing to speed your callback. A fast, simple site wired to instant response and your reviews books more jobs — get found, get picked, get booked.

items.title: Why Followers Are a Vanity Metric for Contractors
items.blurb: Followers are a vanity metric: a number on a screen that doesn't run a service call. Growing a following eats time and rarely turns into paid work. Chase cost per booked job instead. Per 2026 comparisons, Google LSA runs about $168 per booked job versus Angi/HomeAdvisor at about $542.

items.title: Plumbing Marketing Numbers: Real Cost Per Booked Job
items.blurb: For plumbers, Google Local Services Ads run about $57 per lead and book 44.5% into jobs on a $1,714 average ticket — a 6.85x return, per SearchLight 2026. But the real number is cost per booked job, and speed of response beats cheap leads every time.

items.title: Electrical Marketing Numbers: $39 Leads, 8.52x ROAS
items.blurb: Electricians get the cheapest leads in the trades — about $39 each on Google Local Services Ads per SearchLight 2026, versus $58 on regular Search per LocaliQ 2025. Of those, 43.4% book at a $1,434 average ticket, an 8.52x return. The catch: that ticket is the smallest of any trade, so answer fast.

items.title: Roofing Marketing Numbers: Why Leads Cost $79
items.blurb: A roofing lead off Google Search costs about $79, per LocaliQ 2025, nearly double HVAC at ~$45. Roofing is pricey because the jobs are big, urgent, and heavily bid on. But your website converting just 3-7% (WebFX 2026) and slow callbacks waste more money than the lead price ever will.

items.title: Contractor Social Media Setup: Which Accounts Actually Matter
items.blurb: For a one-truck contractor, most social platforms are a waste of time. Set up Google Business Profile and Facebook the right way, keep your name, address, and phone identical everywhere, use a real photo, and skip the rest. Done in an afternoon, not a hobby.

items.title: How to Get Your First 50 Reviews Fast (Exact Texts + Timing)
items.blurb: Ask every past and current customer — plus any friends or family who actually used you — by text, right after the job's done. Send a short message with your direct review link. BrightLocal 2025 says 91% read local reviews and most skip anyone under 4 stars. Stars get you the call.

items.title: First 30 Days, Almost No Money: A Contractor's Warm-Up Plan
items.blurb: Spend your first 30 days on free wins, not ads. Answer fast, fill out your Google Business Profile, and ask every happy customer for a review. These moves cost time, not money, and they decide whether people pick you before you ever pay for a single lead.

items.title: Which Ad Platform Fits Your Trade: Google Search vs LSA vs Meta
items.blurb: Start where intent is highest. Google Local Service Ads books the cheapest jobs at about $168 each (SearchLight Digital 2026) because the lead is exclusive and ready to hire. Use Google Search next for jobs LSA doesn't cover. Save Meta for demand you have to create, not catch.

items.title: Your First Google Search Ad for a Contractor (Without Lighting Money on Fire)
items.blurb: Run a small, tight Google Search ad for the work you actually want. Use exact local keywords, send clicks to a page that matches, answer every call fast, and track cost per booked job, not clicks. Kill the keywords that cost money and book nothing. The average click runs $6.59 (LocaliQ 2025).

items.title: Your First Meta Ad: When Facebook Beats Google for Contractors (and When It Doesn't)
items.blurb: Meta (Facebook/Instagram) ads work when you're building name recognition, retargeting past website visitors, or showing off before-and-after photos. Google Search wins when someone already wants the work done right now. New to ads? Start with Google for emergency trades, then add Meta later for big, planned, picture-heavy jobs.

items.title: Truck Wraps: The Rolling-Billboard Math (and How to Actually Measure It)
items.blurb: A truck wrap is a billboard that drives to the job. It builds memory over time — it does not ring your phone today. The wraps that get calls have a big number, one trade, and your town. To know if it works, put a dedicated tracking number on it. No tracking, no real answer.

items.title: Billboards for Contractors: When They Pay Off and When They're a Vanity Buy
items.blurb: A billboard builds name recognition over months; it does not make your phone ring this week. It pays off for big-ticket, slow-decision trades on one busy road. It's a vanity buy for small urgent jobs. Either way, put a tracking phone number on it — no tracking, no proof.

items.title: Vanity Numbers and Direct Mailers: Do They Still Book Jobs?
items.blurb: Vanity numbers and direct mailers can still book real work for contractors, but only if you track them. Put a unique phone number on each one, count what comes in, and compare the cost per booked job to what your other channels cost. No tracking means no idea, and probably wasted money.

items.title: Why Nobody Clicks Your AI-Generated Poster
items.blurb: Generic AI posters get ignored because they look like the thousand other robot posts people already scroll past. The average contractor site loses 98% of visitors without a call (WebFX 2026). The fix: feed AI one real job with real photos and details, let it polish the words, and answer the phone fast.

items.title: AI Prompt Pack for Contractors: Copy-Paste Prompts That Sound Human
items.blurb: A contractor prompt pack is a set of copy-paste AI instructions for review replies, job posts, social captions, and customer FAQs. You fill in the brackets, paste into a free tool, and read it back. The one trick that makes it work: feed the AI your own past messages so it copies your voice.

items.title: Why Home-Service Customers Buy the Way They Do
items.blurb: Home-service customers buy out of fear, not fun — something broke and they're afraid of getting ripped off. So they hire the first pro who answers and feels trustworthy. 78% hire the first contractor to respond (Lead Connect 2026). Speed and trust beat price almost every time.

items.title: The One Trust Signal That Matters Most for the Trades
items.blurb: The homeowner trusts proof, not promises. They read reviews first (91% per BrightLocal 2025) and hire whoever answers first (78% per Lead Connect 2026). So the one signal that matters is being the business with strong reviews that answers fast. Lead with both, every single time.

items.title: The 3-Hours-a-Month Marketing Plan for Contractors Who Hate Marketing
items.blurb: Spend three hours once a month on the three things that actually book jobs: fill out your Google Business Profile, reply to and ask for reviews, and set up a way to answer every lead in five minutes. Skip the website rebuild and the paid lead networks. Work the free channels you already own.

items.title: Your First Content That Isn't Cringe: Jobsite Photos That Book Jobs
items.blurb: Your content feels fake because you wait until the job is spotless, then take one shiny photo. That reads like a brochure. Instead, snap a before-and-after on every job — the messy reality you already work in. Real photos build trust and book jobs. No script, no fancy camera, no hype.

items.title: Do Yard Signs Still Work for Contractors? Placement, Wording, and Tracking the Calls
items.blurb: Yes. A yard sign at a finished job is a free billboard the neighbors already trust, because they can see your work. Keep it dead simple: trade, one phone number, readable from a moving car. Put a trackable number on it so you know which signs actually earn calls.

items.title: The Tracking Method: Know Which Offline Marketing Actually Books Jobs
items.blurb: Put a different phone number, QR code, or web page on every offline asset — sign, truck wrap, flyer — so each one tells you whether it booked work. Online leads track themselves for free; your offline money flies blind until you tag it. Then you cut the losers and double down on winners.

items.title: AI 101 for Contractors: What Actually Helps and What's Just Hype
items.blurb: AI is good at the boring writing that wins jobs: review replies, FAQ answers, and first drafts of website copy. It is bad at picking up the phone, doing the work, or replacing your judgment. Use it to look responsive and easy to hire. That is it.

items.title: Google Local Services Ads: Yes or No? The Honest Verdict
items.blurb: For most home-service pros, yes. Google LSA books jobs at about $168 each (SearchLight Digital 2026), the cheapest of any lead network, and the leads are exclusive, not shared. It only stops making sense when your phone game is broken or your margins are too thin to feed it.

items.title: Marketing Trends Nobody Is Talking About (For Contractors)
items.blurb: The biggest shift nobody's talking about: in 2026, 35% of homeowners start with AI tools like ChatGPT or Gemini, versus 13.6% who Google first. If your shop isn't in the AI's answer, you're invisible. The fix feeds Google and AI at once — plain answers, an accurate profile, and reviews.

items.title: General Contractor Marketing Numbers: Why GC Leads Cost the Most
items.blurb: General contractors pay about $94 for one Google Search lead (LocaliQ 2025 / CallRail) — the most of any home-service trade. Their websites convert just 3.65% of visitors, the lowest measured (WebFX 2026). Big jobs and slow decisions are why. You win by responding fast and stacking trust.

items.title: Painting Contractor Marketing Numbers: What a Booked Job Really Costs (2026)
items.blurb: For painters, lead networks look cheap — Yelp $18-45, Thumbtack $15-60, Angi $25-80, HomeAdvisor $30-75 (PCA 2026). But the real number is cost per booked job: $180-450 (PCA 2026), because shared leads convert at just 6-10% (2026). Exclusive and owned channels pay best.

items.title: How Many Google Reviews You Need to Rank (Real Numbers by Trade)
items.blurb: We scraped the contractors actually ranking on Google Maps. The ones at the top don't have 20 or 30 reviews — they have hundreds. Median review counts ran from 64 (electrical) to 519 (HVAC). Most shops aren't even close, which is exactly why steady review-getting pulls you ahead fast.
